title: Beat-Aligned Mobile Audio Streaming with Virtual Chunks and Native Playback
gist: A senior engineer details a custom mobile audio-streaming engine (virtual MP3 chunking, HTTP range fetches, native C++ playback) built at Colossal for a beat-discovery app, before the company pivoted to agentic commerce. Off-the-shelf players, HLS/DASH, and full downloads all failed the app's constraint of instant, beat-aligned, artifact-free section switching on weak 3G.

insights.claim: Colossal built a mobile beat-discovery marketplace (producers upload beats, artists swipe a personalized ML-ranked feed) before pivoting to agentic commerce.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Signals a startup abandoning a consumer-media product to chase the agentic-commerce wave, an example of the sector's gravitational pull on venture-stage teams.
insights.quote: At Colossal, before the company pivoted to agentic commerce, we were building a mobile beat-discovery app.

themes.theme: Why off-the-shelf streaming fails interactive audio

insights.claim: React Native's bridge adds roughly 5-10 milliseconds of delay plus jitter to a track switch because pausing and starting players must cross the JS bridge, making precise beat-aligned timing unachievable.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that cross-language bridges impose latency floors that rule out RN for timing-critical media, informing build-vs-buy calls for interactive-audio startups.
insights.quote: this track switch added roughly five to ten milliseconds of delay plus jitter

insights.claim: HLS and DASH are optimized for sequential playback and adaptive bitrate but were not designed for rapid non-linear section hopping, and their segment boundaries break MP3 decode context, producing audible artifacts.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Defines the technical gap that interactive-audio products must fill with custom transport, a moat/complexity signal for anyone building in this niche.

insights.claim: Full-file preloading was unviable because a 3MB beat consumed only 2-3 seconds of user attention, requiring ~14 Mbps of sustained throughput against a 500 kbps target, exceeding available bandwidth by roughly 16x.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Quantifies why swipe-heavy media UIs cannot brute-force bandwidth, forcing selective-fetch architectures as a hard constraint.
insights.quote: even the optimistic estimate exceeded the available bandwidth by roughly sixteen times

insights.claim: Storing one encoded MP3 per beat and fetching only prioritized byte ranges via HTTP Range requests avoids the request count, metadata overhead, and cache fragmentation of HLS-style physical segmentation.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A reusable pattern for selective media loading that reduces storage and request overhead versus segment-per-file streaming.
insights.quote: Virtual chunks let a system store one encoded file and fetch only prioritized byte ranges.

insights.claim: Forcing constant bitrate (CBR) during processing kept chunks near-uniform in size, enabling a preallocated buffer pool and effective buffer reuse at runtime.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Illustrates how encoding choices ripple into memory-management simplicity, a design lever for real-time media engines.

insights.claim: Roughly 3.5 seconds per chunk was the optimal balance: smaller chunks improved responsiveness but raised request overhead, while larger chunks wasted throughput on audio users would skip on low-bandwidth links.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A concrete tuning heuristic for chunked media transport under bandwidth constraints.
insights.quote: Around 3.5 seconds was the best balance for this interaction model.

insights.claim: MP3 bit-reservoir behavior means the first samples of a chunk depend on prior frames, so nine overlap warm-up frames were prepended to every non-initial chunk and discarded after decode to avoid audible artifacts.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A codec-boundary gotcha that any selective-decoding pipeline must handle, generalizable to segmented decode across formats.
insights.quote: If I skipped that overlap warm-up, chunk starts produced audible artifacts on real devices.

insights.claim: A custom 12-byte-per-chunk binary descriptor was chosen over Protobuf or MessagePack because the record shape was fixed and it gave exact control over byte layout with no serialization dependency.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Shows when hand-rolled binary formats beat standard serializers for mobile transport, though the author notes Protobuf would win at higher schema complexity.

insights.claim: Timing-critical playback was implemented in native C++ on top of Superpowered and packaged as an Expo module, keeping state transitions and buffer writes off the React Native bridge to avoid JS runtime pauses.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Reinforces that real-time media on mobile still demands native code despite cross-platform JS frameworks, a durable constraint for the RN ecosystem.

insights.claim: Track and section switches were scheduled to occur at the next bar boundary inside the native control loop rather than executed immediately on user input, to keep transitions rhythmically correct and artifact-free.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Frames beat-aligned switching as a scheduling problem, a mental model transferable to any interactive-audio product.
insights.quote: Beat-aligned switching is a scheduling problem as much as a buffering problem

insights.claim: Cross-platform HTTP transport was built on libcurl in shared C++ to avoid maintaining separate iOS and Android networking stacks and prevent behavior drift over time.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A pattern for keeping mobile transport logic unified, reducing per-platform maintenance surface.

insights.claim: The runtime split work across three thread classes (real-time audio callback, network/decode workers, control logic), keeping allocations, decode, and locks off the audio thread because even small stalls cause audible dropouts.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: The canonical real-time audio threading discipline, applicable to any low-latency signal pipeline.
insights.quote: The audio thread had to remain real-time safe.

insights.claim: Keeping current, previous, and upcoming players warm plus reusing per-track downloader connections eliminated player initialization and connection-setup overhead at the moment of a switch.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Concrete latency-hiding tactics for instant media switching under constrained networks.
insights.locator: First, I kept multiple players warm

insights.claim: A deterministic prefetcher ranked a small set of likely next playback paths (prioritizing same-section-of-next-beat when section lock is on, first-section-of-next-beat when off) instead of downloading everything, avoiding preload queues invalidated by swipes.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: Demonstrates prioritizing bandwidth around a constrained action set, a pattern for resource-limited predictive fetching.

insights.claim: The team deliberately chose deterministic prefetching over a probabilistic/learned model because they lacked sufficient behavior data, making the system understandable and testable before ML could add value.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A pragmatic sequencing principle: ship deterministic heuristics first, add ML once data accrues, relevant to how startups stage ML investment.
insights.quote: Deterministic prefetching based on a user's likely next actions is a practical first step before introducing more complex behavior-driven predictive models.

insights.claim: The author explicitly disclaims benchmark validity, stating no production telemetry was preserved and validation was only development-stage testing under throttled 3G-class conditions.
insights.speaker: Vladyslav Melnychenko
insights.so_what: A credibility marker; readers should treat the design as a pattern reference, not a benchmarked product.
insights.quote: I do not have preserved production telemetry or benchmark logs for this project
